reasoning:
The question asks whether the word 'tariff' or 'tariffs' will be omitted from the digital front pages of NYT and WSJ on AT LEAST ONE single day over a 42-day period (May 20 to June 30, 2025). To resolve NO, the word must appear on EVERY single day on at least one of the pages if we consider Sundays (where WSJ lacks a print edition, placing the burden entirely on the NYT's print index).

While Trump's April 2025 tariff announcements ensure the topic is highly relevant, sustaining a daily mention of a specific policy word—rather than terms like 'trade,' 'taxes,' or broad economic descriptors—in the exact headlines and short summaries for 42 consecutive days is exceedingly difficult. The volume of text on these digital index pages is limited (typically 1,500-3,000 words across all headlines/blurbs). 

Furthermore, the resolution criteria specify that any permanent 'Tariff Tracker' or static section would be excluded, requiring the word to continuously appear in dynamic news sections. Normal news cycle realities, including weekends, major unrelated events (such as late-June Supreme Court decisions), and holidays, strongly suggest there will be at least one day when neither NYT nor WSJ includes the exact word 'tariff(s)' on their front page URL. Thus, the probability of YES is extremely high.
